title: SEGMENT 3 — Mylotarg — proof of concept, withdrawal, and resurrection

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — Mylotarg — proof of concept, withdrawal, and resurrection
The first ADC ever approved was a drug called gemtuzumab ozogamicin, marketed as Mylotarg.
It was approved by the FDA in May of 2000, which makes it older than YouTube, older than the iPod, older than most of you in this room.
And its story is the foundational drama of the entire field — concept proven, drug withdrawn, drug brought back.
The Mylotarg arc is, in microcosm, the whole intellectual history of how this field learned what an ADC needs to be.
The target was CD33, a surface protein that sits on most acute myeloid leukemia cells.
The antibody was a humanized anti-CD33 monoclonal.

The payload was calicheamicin gamma-1, which is one of the most toxic small molecules ever isolated — it's a natural product from a soil bacterium, and what it does is bind to DNA in the minor groove and cleave both strands of the double helix simultaneously, generating breaks that are essentially unrepairable.
It is a molecular guillotine for DNA.
The linker was a hydrazone — a chemical bond designed to be stable at neutral pH and break under the acidic conditions of the lysosome.
You can see the logic.
CD33 was a beautiful target, exquisitely AML-specific.
Calicheamicin was monstrously potent.
The acid-labile linker would release the payload inside the lysosome after internalization.
It was supposed to be a triumph.
And in 2000, it received accelerated approval for relapsed AML in older patients, based on a single-arm trial showing that about thirty percent of patients had some kind of response.
Then it started to go wrong.
The first problem was that the linker wasn't stable enough.
Hydrazones break not just in lysosomes but also, slowly, in the bloodstream.
So a meaningful fraction of patients had calicheamicin leaking off the antibody while the drug was still in circulation, leading to systemic toxicity that the design was supposed to prevent.
The second problem was the drug-antibody ratio — the number of calicheamicin molecules conjugated to each antibody.
Mylotarg was made by a random conjugation chemistry that produced a mixture: some antibodies had no payload, some had one or two, some had four or five, and a substantial fraction were unconjugated.
The unconjugated antibody competed for CD33 binding without delivering any payload — essentially diluting out the active drug.
We didn't fully understand this at the time.
The third problem, the one that actually killed it, was a confirmatory trial called SWOG-S0106.
This was supposed to confirm the benefit of Mylotarg in newly diagnosed AML patients, added on top of standard chemotherapy.

The trial showed no survival benefit, and worse — patients on the Mylotarg arm had higher rates of treatment-related deaths, primarily from a condition called veno-occlusive disease of the liver, which is a severe and sometimes fatal complication.
In 2010, Pfizer pulled Mylotarg from the market voluntarily.
The first ADC, the proof of concept, had been withdrawn.
For a few years, ADCs were in the doghouse.

The field had its early big success — its proof that you could in principle conjugate a poison to an antibody and get tumor selectivity — and then it had its first big failure, a withdrawal that suggested maybe the concept was fatally flawed.
But here's the twist.
Investigators didn't give up on Mylotarg.
They went back and looked carefully at the data, and they realized something important: at lower doses, given on different schedules, the drug worked.
The issue had been dosing — the original approved dose was nine milligrams per square meter, given once.
That was too much, too fast.
When researchers in Europe and Japan tested fractionated dosing — three milligrams per square meter given on three separate days — the toxicity dropped dramatically and the efficacy held.
Several trials in newly diagnosed AML, particularly in patients with favorable cytogenetics, showed real survival benefits when Mylotarg was added to chemotherapy at this lower fractionated dose.
So in 2017, after seven years off the market, the FDA brought Mylotarg back.
Reapproved for AML, with a revised dosing schedule.
The drug that had been the first ADC to be approved, and the first to be withdrawn, became also the first to be reapproved.
The lessons from Mylotarg shape every ADC developed since.
Lesson one: linker stability matters enormously.
A linker that leaks in circulation makes the whole strategy fail.
Lesson two: the drug-antibody ratio matters, and heterogeneous DAR mixtures with lots of unconjugated antibody are a problem.
Lesson three: dose and schedule are not afterthoughts.
The therapeutic window of an ADC is narrow, and finding the right dose is critical.
Lesson four: payload toxicity profiles need to be understood.
Calicheamicin's tendency to cause hepatic veno-occlusive disease was a feature of that particular payload, and every subsequent payload class has been profiled for its own characteristic toxicities.
Mylotarg was the canary in the coal mine.
It told us the concept worked.
It told us the execution mattered.
And every ADC that came after — every drug we're about to talk about — was designed with the Mylotarg lessons in mind.
